items.mistake: Picking k as the value with minimum inertia
items.fix: Use the elbow method, silhouette peaks, stability, and domain constraints; inertia always decreases as k grows toward n.
items.why: Minimum inertia at k = n gives each point its own cluster—inertia zero but useless partitions.
items.example: On 500 points, inertia at k=500 is 0.0 while k=4 may capture 90% of the elbow gain with actionable segments.

items.mistake: Computing external metrics without ground-truth labels
items.fix: Use internal metrics only when labels are absent; reserve ARI, NMI, homogeneity, and completeness for labeled evaluation sets.
items.why: External scores are undefined without a reference partition; misapplying them invites false confidence.
items.example: Calling `adjusted_rand_score` on cluster IDs vs another clustering output measures agreement, not truth—label one side y_true explicitly.

items.mistake: Evaluating clusters on unscaled raw features
items.fix: Apply the same scaling used during clustering before computing silhouette or inertia.
items.why: Distance-based metrics inherit feature-scale artifacts; evaluation geometry must match fitting geometry.
items.example: Income in dollars and age in years produce silhouette dominated by income unless standardized first.

items.mistake: Tuning k on the same labels used for external evaluation
items.fix: Select k with internal metrics and stability; report external scores on held-out labeled data or pre-declared benchmarks.
items.why: Optimizing ARI on the evaluation labels overfits the benchmark, like tuning on the test set in supervised learning.
items.example: Grid-search k to maximize ARI on a 200-patient labeled set, then report that same ARI as unbiased performance.
